name: Podium base layout
description: Floor-layout strategy that pairs a wide, tall ground floor dedicated to public or commercial use with a smaller upper volume — a tower, attic or set-back storey — stacked on top of it. The ground floor reads as a podium or stylobate: it is markedly taller than the floors above (5 to 7 blocks of clear height), occupies the full site footprint and houses the shop front, temple cella, tavern hall or townhouse parlour. The upper volume covers only 30-70% of the podium footprint, producing a setback that lets the lower roof double as a terrace, cornice or visible base. The pattern is the workhorse of classical temples on stylobates, of urban townhouses and shops-with-apartments where the trade floor faces the street and the dwelling sits above, and of any building where commerce and habitation need to share one plot.
placement_rules: Reserve the full site footprint for the ground floor and give it 5 to 7 blocks of clear interior height so it reads as a tall public base rather than an ordinary storey.
Size the upper volume between 30% and 70% of the ground-floor footprint; below 30% the upper looks like a chimney stub, above 70% the podium silhouette is lost.

Choose a single setback strategy per building — either uniform on all four sides (classical podium) or pulled back to one corner (urban townhouse with terrace) — and apply it consistently across every storey above the base.
Treat the exposed roof of the podium as a usable surface: terrace, balustraded walkway, cornice band or planted parapet, never raw shingles, so the setback is read as deliberate.
Place the main public entrance on the long facade of the podium at ground level and align it with the centroid of the ground floor; the upper volume must not block or overhang the entrance approach.
Stack the upper volume directly over load-bearing podium walls — typically the rear and one side — so the structural logic of the setback reads from outside.
Mark the transition between podium and upper volume with a horizontal string course, cornice or change of material spanning the full perimeter, so the two volumes are legibly distinct.
lighting_ventilation: The tall ground floor admits light through large street-facing windows or an open shop front on the long facade; high clerestory windows along the rear can supplement the front-lit hall. The upper volume sits free of the podium edge on at least one side, so its rooms gain windows on three or four faces and cross-ventilate easily. The exposed podium roof acts as a light shelf reflecting daylight up into the upper storey's windows.
